title: Share of adults who are obese vs. daily protein supply
subtitle: Obesity is defined as having a body-mass index (BMI) equal to, or greater than, 30. BMI is a person's weight (in kilograms) divided by their height (in meters) squared.
caption:
Share of adults who are obese vs. daily protein supply

Obesity is defined as having a body-mass index (BMI) equal to, or greater than, 30. BMI is a person's weight (in kilograms) divided by their height (in meters) squared.

This scatter plot maps the relationship between daily protein supply in grams per day per capita on the horizontal axis and the share of adults who are obese as a percentage on the vertical axis for the year 2022. The data points are color-coded by region, distinguishing North America, South America, Africa, Europe, Asia, and Oceania. A general positive trend is visible, where countries with higher protein supplies tend to have higher obesity rates, though the distribution is wide. At the lower end of the protein scale, between 30 and 60 grams, countries such as Burundi, Somalia, and Bangladesh cluster with obesity rates generally below 15%. In the middle range, around 80 to 100 grams, nations like Jordan, Syria, and the global average sit with obesity shares roughly between 15% and 30%. The upper right quadrant contains countries with protein supplies exceeding 120 grams, including the United States, Ireland, and Iceland, where obesity rates vary significantly from approximately 20% to over 40%. Distinct outliers appear at the top of the chart; Nauru, Tonga, and Samoa exhibit obesity rates between 60% and 70% despite having protein supplies ranging from roughly 105 to 130 grams. Conversely, China appears with a protein supply near 130 grams but an obesity rate below 10%. European nations generally occupy the right side of the chart with high protein intake, while their obesity rates span a broad band from roughly 15% to 30%.
